skill.name: fullstack-dev
skill.description: Guides full-stack application development across backend architecture, API design, frontend integration, authentication, testing, and production readiness.
skill.summary: Build full-stack applications with structured backend, API, auth, testing, and release patterns.
skill.author: MiniMax-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and cryptography patterns, but most are markdown examples for normal full-stack development and several high-severity alerts are keyword false positives. No prompt-injection text or malicious exfiltration intent was found. One upload example uses a user-controlled filename in a storage key, so publication should include a warning and the example should be hardened.

medium_findings.title: Unsanitized Filename in Upload Key Example
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The upload example reads filename from the request query and appends it directly to a storage key. If copied into production, a crafted filename could create confusing object keys, unsafe names, or downstream path handling issues. This appears to be an instructional weakness, not malicious behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines show direct use of a user-controlled filename in a generated upload key. Confidence is high for the unsafe pattern, but lower than critical because the file is markdown guidance and S3 keys do not imply local filesystem traversal by themselves.

low_findings.title: Command Execution Alerts Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command findings are primarily fenced markdown examples such as npm build checks and curl smoke tests. They do not execute automatically, do not use untrusted interpolation, and fit the stated full-stack development purpose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is displayed as shell or test examples inside documentation. No executable script, installer hook, or hidden command runner was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Environment and Token Handling Examples Need Care
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill includes examples for environment variables, database URLs, JWT secrets, bearer tokens, cookies, and CORS credentials. These are legitimate full-stack topics, and the text repeatedly warns against hardcoded secrets and localStorage tokens, but copied code must still be reviewed for deployment safety.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sensitive patterns are real but expected for an authentication and configuration guide. Confidence is high that they are legitimate examples rather than credential harvesting because they use local or configurable application endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, C2, and SAM Alerts Are Keyword Collisions
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography hits include a checklist saying not to use MD5 or SHA for passwords and a PostgreSQL hash index table. The C2 and Windows SAM detections map to hosting and SQL row-level-security text, not malware infrastructure or credential database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines show defensive checklist text, database documentation, and hosting options. There is no evidence of weak password hashing recommendations, C2 behavior, or Windows SAM database access.

low_findings.title: Critical Heuristic Combination Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The analyzer combined code, network, and credential patterns across separate documentation examples. I did not find evidence of a single flow that executes code, reads secrets, and exfiltrates them over the network.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The risk factors exist in the corpus, but they are separated across normal guide sections. No prompt injection, hidden automation, obfuscation, or malicious data flow was found during contextual review.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsanitized Filename in Upload Key Example
dangerous_patterns.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The upload example reads filename from the request query and appends it directly to a storage key. If copied into production, a crafted filename could create confusing object keys, unsafe names, or downstream path handling issues. This appears to be an instructional weakness, not malicious behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines show direct use of a user-controlled filename in a generated upload key. Confidence is high for the unsafe pattern, but lower than critical because the file is markdown guidance and S3 keys do not imply local filesystem traversal by themselves.

content.user_title: Build Full-Stack Apps With Backend Patterns
content.value_statement: Full-stack projects often fail at boundaries between backend, frontend, data, auth, and deployment. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured patterns for building, testing, and hardening those boundaries.
actual_capabilities: Guides feature-first project structure with controller, service, and repository boundaries.
Provides API client, REST endpoint, pagination, error, and OpenAPI design patterns.
Covers authentication flows, JWT rules, refresh tokens, RBAC, CORS, and cookie handling.
Explains database access, migrations, indexing, multi-tenancy, and schema decision patterns.
Includes testing, release, environment, logging, file upload, and real-time integration checklists.
limitations: It is a guidance skill, not a generator with verified runnable starter templates.
Code examples require adaptation to the selected language, framework, and infrastructure.
Security-sensitive examples need project-specific review before production use.
It does not replace official framework, cloud provider, or compliance documentation.

use_cases.title: Scaffold a New Product Backend
use_cases.description: Plan the stack, folder structure, API boundaries, configuration, errors, logging, database access, and first build checks.

use_cases.title: Integrate Frontend and API Safely
use_cases.description: Choose an API client pattern, configure base URLs, attach auth credentials, handle errors, and manage loading states.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Service for Release
use_cases.description: Review testing, migrations, environment variables, health checks, CORS, observability, and production hardening before launch.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Simple App
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a small full-stack app with a frontend, backend API, database, and authentication. Ask only for missing stack choices.

prompt_templates.title: Design an API Boundary
prompt_templates.prompt: Design REST endpoints, request validation, error responses, pagination, and frontend API client patterns for this feature: [describe feature].

prompt_templates.title: Review an Existing Stack
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this full-stack architecture for service boundaries, config, auth, database access, testing, and production risks: [paste summary].

prompt_templates.title: Harden Before Release
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a release readiness checklist for this service, including migrations, secrets, CORS, observability, tests, rollback, and smoke checks.

output_examples.input: Build a todo app with React, Express, PostgreSQL, and JWT auth.
output: A stack decision summary with feature-first folders and service boundaries.
A REST API plan with validation, typed errors, auth middleware, and database repositories.
A frontend integration plan with API client setup, loading states, token refresh, and build checks.
A release checklist covering tests, migrations, secrets, CORS, logging, health checks, and rollback.
A risk summary that separates blockers from follow-up improvements.
A handoff list with key files, run commands, and missing production work.

best_practices: Start by choosing the stack, service type, database, integration style, real-time needs, and auth model.
Keep business logic out of controllers and centralize configuration, validation, error handling, and logging.
Run build, smoke, integration, auth, and release checks before calling the work complete.
anti_patterns: Do not copy security-sensitive examples without adapting secrets, origins, token storage, and upload validation.
Do not mix business logic, database queries, and HTTP response handling in the same layer.
Do not hardcode API URLs, credentials, CORS wildcards, or environment-specific values in production code.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design and implement full-stack applications with backend services, APIs, frontend integration, auth, testing, and release checks.

faq.question: Does it support multiple stacks?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for Node.js, Python, Go, Django, React, Next.js, REST, GraphQL, tRPC, and related patterns.

faq.question: Can I use it for pure frontend styling?
faq.answer: No. The skill says not to use it for pure frontend UI, CSS, or styling work without backend context.

faq.question: Does it include production guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers health checks, CORS, environment variables, logging, graceful shutdown, migrations, testing, and release readiness.

faq.question: Are the examples safe to copy directly?
faq.answer: No. Treat them as patterns. Review security-sensitive code for your framework, deployment environment, and threat model.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk medium?
faq.answer: Most static alerts are documentation false positives, but one upload example uses an unsanitized filename in a generated storage key.
